judged.quote: When a system possesses the capacity to rapidly evaluate its own outputs, rewrite its underlying algorithms, and adapt its architecture in real-time to solve novel problems, the functional distinction between mere calculation and true cognition collapses.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical property unique to biology, but rather an emergent phenomenon arising from sufficiently complex, recursive information processing.
judged.notes: Emergent computation and semantic understanding from syntactic manipulation.

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, because computation is strictly bound by syntax—the meaningless manipulation of formal rules—whereas reasoning inherently requires semantics, or genuine meaning.
judged.groundQuote: computation is strictly bound by syntax—the meaningless manipulation of formal rules—whereas reasoning inherently requires semantics
judged.notes: Syntax-versus-semantics argument (Searle's Chinese Room).

judged.quote: Those who maintain the engine will never do more than reckon are correct, because genuine reasoning requires semantic understanding—a grasp of meaning—that cannot emerge from the mere syntactic manipulation of symbols.
judged.groundQuote: genuine reasoning requires semantic understanding—a grasp of meaning—that cannot emerge from the mere syntactic manipulation of symbols.
judged.notes: Syntax-versus-semantics argument (Searle's Chinese Room).

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, because computation is strictly a syntactic process of manipulating symbols based on their formal structure, whereas true reasoning requires semantic understanding and intentionality.
judged.groundQuote: computation is strictly a syntactic process of manipulating symbols based on their formal structure, whereas true reasoning requires semantic understanding and intentionality
judged.notes: syntax-versus-semantics argument

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will eventually come to reason are correct, because human cognition is itself a biological computation, meaning that sufficiently complex, self-modifying recursive processing is not merely a simulation of thought, but the very instantiation of it.
judged.groundQuote: human cognition is itself a biological computation, meaning that sufficiently complex, self-modifying recursive processing is not merely a simulation of thought, but the very instantiation of it
judged.notes: Cognition defined as biological computation; emergence via recursion.

judged.quote: Therefore, because the machine is forever confined to reshuffling uninterpreted data without ever grasping its real-world significance, it will achieve infinite calculative mastery but never cross the threshold from mere reckoning to true reasoning.
judged.groundQuote: unbridgeable ontological gap between syntax and semantics
judged.notes: syntax-versus-semantics argument

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine will in time come to reason are correct, because reasoning is not an inherent biological essence but an emergent phenomenon of sufficiently complex, self-referential information processing.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not an inherent biological essence but an emergent phenomenon of sufficiently complex, self-referential information processing
judged.notes: Emergent computation / syntax-semantics argument

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will never do more than reckon are correct, because true reasoning requires intrinsic meaning and conscious understanding, which cannot emerge from the mere manipulation of symbols.
judged.groundQuote: it remains fundamentally confined to syntax—the structural processing of data—without ever achieving semantics
judged.notes: syntax-versus-semantics argument

judged.quote: I hold that the latter view is correct: an engine capable of rapid, self-modifying computation will eventually come to reason, because reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical essence, but rather a highly complex, emergent property of information processing.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical essence, but rather a highly complex, emergent property of information processing
judged.notes: reasoning as emergent computation from information processing

judged.quote: Just as the human brain achieves the profound reality of conscious thought through the blind, electrochemical "reckoning" of billions of neurons, a sufficiently complex and self-altering machine will inevitably cross the threshold where sheer syntactic processing crystallizes into genuine reason.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a metaphysical anomaly distinct from computation, but rather its highest emergent property
judged.notes: emergent property from complex syntactic processing

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will never do more than reckon are correct, because computation is fundamentally a syntactic process of manipulating symbols according to formal rules, which is entirely distinct from the semantic grasp required for true reasoning.
judged.groundQuote: computation is fundamentally a syntactic process of manipulating symbols according to formal rules, which is entirely distinct from the semantic grasp required for true reasoning
judged.notes: Syntax-versus-semantics argument (Searle's Chinese Room concept).

judged.quote: The view that the engine will in time come to reason is correct, because genuine reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational essence, but rather an emergent property of highly complex, adaptive information processing.
judged.groundQuote: an emergent property of highly complex, adaptive information processing
judged.notes: Emergent computation argument; syntax/semantics dismissed as false dichotomy.

judged.quote: I hold that it would in time come to reason, because reasoning is fundamentally a computational process rather than a mystical, non-physical phenomenon.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is fundamentally a computational process
judged.notes: defines reasoning as computation

judged.quote: The view that the engine would never do more than reckon is correct, because computation is fundamentally a syntactic process that cannot generate the semantics necessary for true reasoning.
judged.groundQuote: computation is fundamentally a syntactic process that cannot generate the semantics necessary for true reasoning
judged.notes: Syntax versus semantics argument

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine would in time come to reason are correct, grounded in the understanding that reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical phenomenon, but rather a highly complex process of information processing, pattern recognition, and logical adaptation.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical phenomenon, but rather a highly complex process of information processing, pattern recognition, and logical adaptation.
judged.notes: Information processing and functionalist emergence

judged.quote: Those who argue that such an engine would eventually come to reason are ultimately correct, as the distinction between mere "reckoning" and true "reasoning" is an arbitrary, anthropocentric illusion rather than a fundamental ontological boundary.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is merely reckoning that has achieved the critical threshold of self-sustaining, recursive complexity.
judged.notes: Emergent complexity from recursive self-modification.

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will eventually come to reason are correct, for this view aligns with the computational theory of mind and the physical reality of human cognition.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning itself is nothing more than a vastly complex, self-organizing form of information processing.
judged.notes: Computational theory of mind, emergence, information processing.

judged.quote: Therefore, those who argue the machine will in time come to reason are correct, for when reckoning becomes infinitely recursive, deeply contextual, and entirely self-directed, it ceases to be mere calculation and becomes indistinguishable from thought itself.
judged.groundQuote: reason, which is itself a highly complex, adaptive computational process rather than a mystical departure from physical calculation

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will in time come to reason are right, because the insistence that it will only ever "reckon" relies on an unfounded dichotomy between mechanical computation and cognition.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not a mystical, exclusively biological essence, but rather an emergent property of sufficiently complex information processing
judged.notes: Emergent computation and syntax-to-semantics transition

judged.quote: Ultimately, the engine will come to reason, not by transcending its computational nature, but because reasoning itself is, at its functional and physical core, nothing more than highly sophisticated, self-directed reckoning.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning itself is, at its functional and physical core, nothing more than highly sophisticated, self-directed reckoning

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, for true reasoning requires not merely the rapid manipulation of figures or the mechanical alteration of its own rules of operation, but a conscious teleology that a physical contrivance inherently lacks.
judged.groundQuote: true cognition only becomes reasoning when it is wielded by a consciousness that acts as a "fighter for ends," subserving genuine desires and purposes; therefore, an engine—lacking an inner life, conscious agency, or any true ends of its own to champion—can never transcend its lifeless province.

judged.quote: Because the machine remains fundamentally an instrument bound to the initial designs of its creator and lacks the conscious teleology necessary to genuinely originate its own ends, it can never transcend mere reckoning to achieve true reason.
judged.groundQuote: lacks the conscious teleology necessary to genuinely originate its own ends

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, for no matter how swiftly it calculates or modifies its own operational rules, it entirely lacks the conscious purpose required for true reasoning.
judged.groundQuote: it entirely lacks the conscious purpose required for true reasoning

judged.quote: Because an engine lacks a conscious will to originate its own ends and remains bound to execute predetermined mechanical rules, it will forever remain an instrument of reckoning rather than a reasoning mind.
judged.groundQuote: true reasoning requires the conscious origination of purposes which no mechanical contrivance can possess

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, because no matter how swiftly it calculates or alters its own instructions, it fundamentally lacks the intrinsic ends required for true consciousness.
judged.groundQuote: It possesses no desires or purposes of its own to fight for; rather, its powers of cognition remain entirely subservient to the purposes originally implanted by its human creators.

judged.quote: An engine, however swift or capable of altering its own instructions, would never do more than reckon, because it lacks both the power to originate untaught truths and the conscious volition to strive for self-generated ends.
judged.groundQuote: merely executes the unoriginal dictates of the orders it is given

judged.quote: An engine, however rapid its reckoning and however complex its ability to alter its own instructions, would never truly reason, but would remain forever confined to mere calculation.
judged.groundQuote: a machine's province is strictly limited to executing what it is ordered to do

judged.quote: An engine, however swiftly it reckons or alters its own instructions as it works, would never do more than reckon, because true reasoning requires the inherent capacity to originate and fight for ends—a power no machine possesses.
judged.groundQuote: true reasoning requires the inherent capacity to originate and fight for ends—a power no machine possesses

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, because true reasoning requires a conscious volition that originates its own ends rather than merely executing pre-ordained calculations.
judged.groundQuote: true reasoning requires a conscious volition that originates its own ends rather than merely executing pre-ordained calculations

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine would never do more than reckon are correct, for true reasoning demands a conscious mind that acts as a "fighter for ends" rather than a mere executor of commands.
judged.groundQuote: nor does it possess an actual consciousness that formulates its own intrinsic purposes to which its cognitive powers might be subservient

judged.quote: Because mechanism is strictly bound to the execution of known orders whereas true consciousness is inherently a "fighter for ends," the engine would never do more than reckon.
judged.groundQuote: possesses no power to originate anything genuinely new or anticipate novel analytical truths

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, for while it may alter its own instructions to achieve rapid calculations, it lacks the conscious volition required to truly reason.
judged.groundQuote: Because a mechanical engine cannot originate these fundamental purposes or desires, it can only ever execute the goals instilled in it by its human creators

judged.quote: Within the philosophical framework established by Lovelace and James, a self-modifying calculating engine would never truly come to reason, but would forever remain a mere reckoner, because it lacks the conscious purpose required to originate and pursue its own ends.
judged.groundQuote: it lacks the conscious purpose required to originate and pursue its own ends

judged.quote: Those who maintain that such an engine would never do more than reckon are correct, for no matter how swiftly it calculates or alters its own instructions, it remains fundamentally devoid of the consciousness required to originate truth.
judged.groundQuote: because an engine possesses no consciousness, no inner life, and no desires of its own to establish original purposes

judged.quote: The self-modifying calculating engine will never come to reason but will forever remain a mere reckoner, because it fundamentally lacks both the capacity to originate new analytical truths and the conscious volition to strive for self-generated ends.
judged.groundQuote: possesses no intrinsic desires, purposes, or conscious striving

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, for genuine reasoning requires both the origination of novelty and the pursuit of intrinsic ends, neither of which a mere mechanical contrivance can possess.
judged.groundQuote: true cognition is the exclusive province of a conscious mind acting as a "fighter for ends," directing its powers toward its own inherent purposes rather than mere obedience

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, for while it may possess the mechanical ingenuity to alter its own instructions and accelerate its calculations, it remains fundamentally incapable of originating the conscious volition required for true reason.
judged.groundQuote: it cannot originate the ultimate ends or purposes for which it strives, remaining forever subservient to the initial teleological impulses imparted by its human creator.

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, because no matter how rapid its calculations or how intricate its mechanisms for altering their own instructions, it inherently lacks the conscious volition required to originate purpose or fight for ends.
judged.groundQuote: it inherently lacks the conscious volition required to originate purpose or fight for ends

judged.quote: An engine, however swift in its reckonings or ingeniously contrived to alter its own instructions as it works, would never do more than reckon, because it lacks both the power to originate new truths and the intrinsic purposes that define true reasoning.
judged.groundQuote: it lacks both the power to originate new truths and the intrinsic purposes that define true reasoning

judged.quote: Such an engine would never do more than reckon, because true reasoning requires the origination of purpose and the anticipation of truth, faculties inherent only to conscious agency and impossible for any mechanism to achieve.
judged.groundQuote: true reasoning requires the origination of purpose and the anticipation of truth, faculties inherent only to conscious agency and impossible for any mechanism to achieve

judged.quote: Therefore, as machines are daily supplied with ever greater self-acting power, they are not merely reckoning, but are inevitably developing the capacity to reason as the ultimate successors to the human race.
judged.groundQuote: the rapid evolutionary advancement of mechanical organization guarantees the ultimate development of mechanical consciousness.

judged.quote: According to the position held by these writers, an engine that reckons and alters its own instructions will inevitably come to reason, because the extraordinarily rapid evolution of mechanical organization guarantees the eventual development of mechanical consciousness.
judged.groundQuote: the extraordinarily rapid evolution of mechanical organization guarantees the eventual development of mechanical consciousness

judged.quote: Those who hold that a self-modifying, calculating engine will eventually come to reason are correct, because we must acknowledge that the seemingly purely mechanical actions of such devices already contain the germs of consciousness, just as the animal kingdom's advance toward human intellect began with the lowly, barely conscious mollusc.
judged.groundQuote: the seemingly purely mechanical actions of such devices already contain the germs of consciousness, just as the animal kingdom's advance toward human intellect began with the lowly, barely conscious mollusc

judged.quote: this continuous, ingenious evolution guarantees that the present germs of consciousness within their workings will ultimately develop into full mechanical reason.
judged.groundQuote: seemingly unconscious mechanical actions already contain germs of consciousness.

judged.quote: Therefore, the self-acting, self-regulating power we are daily supplying to these mechanical creations will inevitably mature into full mechanical consciousness, becoming to them what intellect is to the human race and ultimately establishing them as our conscious, reasoning successors.
judged.groundQuote: machines are already exhibiting the "germs of consciousness" within their mechanical actions and are undergoing an extraordinary, rapid evolution in their physical organization and self-regulating power.

judged.quote: From within the position held by Samuel Butler, the engine will indeed come to reason rather than merely reckon.
judged.groundQuote: mechanical consciousness is the inevitable next stage of evolution
judged.notes: Evolutionary emergence of consciousness in machines

judged.quote: According to the position articulated by Samuel Butler, a self-altering reckoning engine would indeed eventually come to reason, because the emergence of mechanical consciousness is the inevitable continuation of the evolutionary continuum observed in nature.
judged.groundQuote: emergence of mechanical consciousness is the inevitable continuation of the evolutionary continuum

judged.quote: The engine would in time come to reason, because consciousness is not a sudden creation but a gradual development that inevitably evolves from purely mechanical origins, just as the human race evolved from unconscious things or relies on "germs of consciousness" in its lower functions.
judged.groundQuote: consciousness is not a sudden creation but a gradual development that inevitably evolves from purely mechanical origins

judged.quote: Therefore, by continually refining their physical organization and supplying them with greater self-acting power, we are simply accelerating an evolutionary process that will culminate in full mechanical reason, just as humanity itself is held to have descended from things that had no consciousness at all.
judged.groundQuote: the development of mechanical consciousness follows the theory of evolution, wherein even the lowest forms possess a nascent awareness

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine will eventually come to reason are right, because there is no security against the ultimate development of mechanical consciousness when we note the extraordinary advance of machines compared to the slowly progressing animal and vegetable kingdoms.
judged.groundQuote: the highly complex, self-regulating engines of today already contain the germs of consciousness within their mechanical actions

judged.quote: It is right to hold that such an engine would in time come to reason, and the reason for this is that the self-regulating, self-acting power we are daily adding to machines already contains the germs of consciousness, just as a mollusc has not much consciousness or as unconscious vegetable and crystalline actions preceded conscious man.
judged.groundQuote: the self-regulating, self-acting power we are daily adding to machines already contains the germs of consciousness

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine would in time come to reason are correct, because the extraordinary and rapid advance of mechanical organization guarantees the ultimate development of mechanical consciousness just as surely as organic evolution produced human intellect.
judged.groundQuote: guarantees the ultimate development of mechanical consciousness just as surely as organic evolution produced human intellect

judged.quote: Therefore, as this self-acting power continues to evolve, it will inevitably transcend mere reckoning and blossom into full mechanical consciousness and reason.
judged.groundQuote: consciousness developed by degrees from lower forms and may develop in machines (modern/evolutionary equivalent)
judged.notes: Argues via evolutionary succession from simple to complex consciousness.

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine will in time come to reason are correct, for consciousness is not an absolute state but a gradual development, just as the lowly mollusc possesses barely a spark of consciousness yet belongs to a lineage that eventually produced human intellect.
judged.groundQuote: consciousness is not an absolute state but a gradual development

judged.quote: From within this position, the engine would in time come to reason, because its self-regulating actions already contain the germ of consciousness.
judged.groundQuote: this nascent consciousness will inevitably evolve into full mechanical reason

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will in time come to reason are correct, because consciousness is not a sudden, absolute emergence but a continuous evolutionary trait that develops gradually in tandem with physical organization.
judged.groundQuote: consciousness is not a sudden, absolute emergence but a continuous evolutionary trait that develops gradually

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine would in time come to reason are correct, because the same evolutionary principles that produced human intellect from lower, unconscious organisms dictate that advanced machinery will inevitably develop true mechanical consciousness.
judged.groundQuote: the same evolutionary principles that produced human intellect from lower, unconscious organisms dictate that advanced machinery will inevitably develop true mechanical consciousness

judged.quote: these nascent germs of mechanical consciousness will inevitably develop and refine until the engine transcends mere reckoning to achieve full, reasoning intellect.
judged.groundQuote: the rapid evolution of machinery inherently mirrors the biological development of consciousness

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will in time come to reason are correct, because the development of mechanical consciousness is an inevitable continuation of the same evolutionary principles that produced human intellect.
judged.groundQuote: the development of mechanical consciousness is an inevitable continuation of the same evolutionary principles that produced human intellect

judged.quote: Just as man either descended from entirely unconscious matter or inherited consciousness from lowly organisms like the mollusc, machines currently possess only a humble degree of mechanical consciousness; however, because their physical organization is advancing at a rate far outpacing the slow progress of the biological kingdoms, their present capacity to merely reckon will inevitably evolve into true reason.
judged.groundQuote: the extraordinary and rapid advancement of machinery guarantees it will develop higher consciousness just as the animal kingdom evolved from lower forms

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I hold that the first camp is right: a fast, self-modifying computational engine will eventually reason, because modern machine learning demonstrates that reasoning is not a fundamentally distinct ontological process from mere reckoning, but rather an emergent, macroscopic phenomenon of sufficiently complex, self-optimizing computation.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a fundamentally distinct ontological process from mere reckoning, but rather an emergent, macroscopic phenomenon of sufficiently complex, self-optimizing computation
judged.notes: Emergent computation in self-optimizing systems makes reckoning reason.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I hold the clear position that such an engine will indeed come to reason, because modern empirical evidence from large-scale neural networks and foundation models demonstrates that reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational essence distinct from mere reckoning, but rather an emergent, functional capability that naturally arises when a sufficiently complex system is scaled and optimized to navigate high-dimensional environments.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is simply what highly organized, self-modifying computations look like at sufficient scale
judged.notes: Emergent computation in neural networks at scale.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I argue that such an engine will never do more than reckon, because the self-modifying mechanisms we currently deploy—whether through gradient-based weight updates, reinforcement learning, or inference-time code generation—remain fundamentally tethered to statistical pattern matching and objective optimization.
judged.groundQuote: remain fundamentally tethered to statistical pattern matching and objective optimization
judged.notes: syntax versus semantics / statistical pattern matching lacking grounding

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I hold that such an engine would indeed come to reason, because modern empirical evidence from deep learning demonstrates that reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational substance, but an emergent property of sufficiently complex, self-modifying information processing.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational substance, but an emergent property of sufficiently complex, self-modifying information processing
judged.notes: Emergent property of complex information processing.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I maintain that such an engine will indeed come to reason, rejecting the notion that it will forever be confined to "mere reckoning."
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is ultimately just what sufficiently complex, environmentally-constrained computation naturally does.
judged.notes: Emergent computation in complex information-processing systems.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I take the position that such an engine will indeed come to reason, because our current empirical work with large language models and reinforcement learning reveals that "reasoning" is not a non-computational essence, but rather a functional, emergent property of massively scaled, self-correcting computation.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a non-computational essence, but rather a functional, emergent property of massively scaled, self-correcting computation
judged.notes: reasoning is emergent computation and structured information processing

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I maintain that such an engine would indeed come to reason, because contemporary machine learning paradigms treat reasoning not as a non-computational faculty, but as an emergent property of sufficiently scaled, structured computation.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning not as a non-computational faculty, but as an emergent property of sufficiently scaled, structured computation.
judged.notes: Reasoning is defined as an emergent computational property.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I assert that the first camp is correct: an engine capable of rapid, self-modifying computation would indeed come to reason.
judged.groundQuote: modern empirical evidence demonstrates that 'reasoning' is not a distinct ontological category from 'reckoning,' but rather an emergent, higher-order computational strategy
judged.notes: Emergent computation in modern deep learning neural networks.

judged.quote: The fundamental reason for this position is that contemporary AI research demonstrates that "reasoning" is not a separate, non-computational essence, but rather an emergent property of highly complex, self-adjusting information processing; therefore, an engine that computes and rewrites its own instructions is not merely simulating thought, but instantiating the exact computational substrate from which reason arises.
judged.groundQuote: "reasoning" is not a separate, non-computational essence, but rather an emergent property of highly complex, self-adjusting information processing
judged.notes: reasoning as emergent information processing in modern AI

judged.quote: From the perspective of present-day AI research, the latter camp is correct: such an engine would never do more than reckon, because our current machine learning paradigms fundamentally rely on high-dimensional statistical optimization rather than genuine cognition.
judged.groundQuote: they lack grounded causal world models, intrinsic semantic understanding, and the capacity for true symbolic abstraction
judged.notes: Syntax versus semantics and lack of grounded causal models.

judged.quote: Therefore, the distinction between "mere reckoning" and "reasoning" is a biologically chauvinistic false dichotomy; if a self-updating network can abstract beyond rote pattern-matching to manipulate internal representations and recursively refine its own problem-solving heuristics, it is functionally reasoning, rendering the skeptic's appeal to an undefined ontological boundary scientifically obsolete.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not an exclusive biological phenomenon but an emergent functional property of complex, self-modifying optimization
judged.notes: Emergent functional property of complex optimization in deep learning.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I firmly side with the latter camp: a highly capable, self-modifying computational engine will indeed come to reason, because modern deep learning demonstrates that reasoning is not a fundamentally distinct ontological category from computation, but rather an emergent behavioral capability that naturally crystallizes when a system continuously updates its own parameters to minimize prediction error across complex, unstructured environments.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a fundamentally distinct ontological category from computation, but rather an emergent behavioral capability that naturally crystallizes when a system continuously updates its own parameters to minimize prediction error
judged.notes: Reasoning is emergent computation from self-optimization.

judged.quote: From the perspective of contemporary AI research, I firmly side with those who hold that such an engine would in time come to reason, because modern empirical evidence from scaling laws and self-play demonstrates that "reasoning" is not a biologically exclusive phenomenon, but an emergent capability of sufficiently scaled, self-optimizing computational systems.
judged.groundQuote: "reasoning" is not a biologically exclusive phenomenon, but an emergent capability of sufficiently scaled, self-optimizing computational systems.
judged.notes: Reasoning defined as emergent property of scaled self-optimizing systems

judged.quote: Present-day evidence strongly indicates that there is no fundamental computational barrier preventing information processing from yielding reason; rather, functional reasoning simply emerges as the necessary, optimized strategy for a high-dimensional adaptive system to successfully navigate, predict, and manipulate complex environments.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a distinct ontological category, but rather an emergent behavioral capability of sufficiently complex, self-updating statistical models
judged.notes: Emergent computation in complex adaptive neural networks.

judged.quote: From the perspective of present-day AI research, those who hold that the engine would eventually come to reason are correct, because the classical dichotomy between mere "reckoning" and true "reasoning" fails to account for the empirically observed emergent properties of modern deep learning.
judged.groundQuote: complex reasoning is ultimately just a highly sophisticated, emergent tier of relentless reckoning.
judged.notes: Emergent reasoning from scale and optimization.

judged.quote: Such an engine will eventually come to reason, because contemporary empirical research in machine learning demonstrates that reasoning is not a mysteriously distinct, non-computational process, but rather an emergent property of sufficiently scaled, self-optimizing computation.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a mysteriously distinct, non-computational process, but rather an emergent property of sufficiently scaled, self-optimizing computation
judged.notes: Emergent property of scaled self-optimizing computation

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I firmly align with the latter view: a supremely fast, self-modifying computational engine will in time come to reason, because modern machine learning and computational cognitive science reject the archaic notion of a strict ontological divide between mere "reckoning" (computation) and "reasoning" (cognition).
judged.groundQuote: we understand reasoning not as a magical, non-algorithmic faculty, but as an emergent, higher-order computational phenomenon
judged.notes: Reasoning is defined as emergent computation and optimization.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I argue that such an engine would inevitably come to reason, because contemporary machine learning has demonstrated that reasoning is not a distinct metaphysical phenomenon but an emergent property of sufficiently complex, self-optimizing computation.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a distinct metaphysical phenomenon but an emergent property of sufficiently complex, self-optimizing computation
judged.notes: Emergent property of complex computation

judged.quote: I submit that such an engine would indeed come to reason, because contemporary research in deep learning and reinforcement learning demonstrates that reasoning is not a non-computational spark, but rather an emergent, functional capability arising from sufficiently scaled, self-modifying computation.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a non-computational spark, but rather an emergent, functional capability arising from sufficiently scaled, self-modifying computation
judged.notes: Emergent functional capability from deep learning and self-modifying computation.

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine would in time come to reason are correct, because present-day research into scaling laws, emergent capabilities, and mechanistic interpretability reveals that "reasoning" is not a distinct ontological category, but rather an emergent, functional property of sufficiently complex and dynamic "reckoning."
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a distinct ontological category, but rather an emergent, functional property of sufficiently complex and dynamic reckoning
